danger_signals.visual_form: Red/depleted need bars in UI; Sim animations show distress
danger_signals.meaning: Character in danger of negative outcome (starvation, exhaustion, poor hygiene)

danger_signals.visual_form: Animated flames on objects/structures; red glow spreading
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental hazard threatening Sim safety and property

danger_signals.visual_form: Dark red thought bubbles; sad/angry Sim facial expressions
danger_signals.meaning: Psychological distress state affecting Sim behavior and performance

safety_signals.visual_form: Bright green UI indicators for hunger, energy, bladder, etc.
Light colored thought bubbles with happy/content symbols; smiling Sim faces
safety_signals.meaning: Psychological wellbeing; Sim in good emotional state

safety_signals.visual_form: Increasing household funds display; visual property upgrades

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Lot size, house quality, object luxury levels distinguish wealthy vs. poor households
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Neighborhood lot placement; house exterior appearance; furniture and decoration quality

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Sim clothing changes to job-specific uniforms; career reward objects reflect profession type
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Career-related items in home (military medals, police uniforms, chef's kitchen)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Bright outdoor lighting; buildings/lots active; some Sims away at work
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Adult Sims leave for work; children attend school; reduced household activity

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Darker environment lighting; interior home lighting becomes primary focus
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Sims return home; social interactions increase; sleep cycles activate

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Same time-of-day lighting but altered lot activity patterns
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Children remain home; adults have free time; increased social gatherings possible

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Sim character model transitions through seven life stages (infancy to old age); visual aging of appearance
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Life-stage-specific interactions become available; capability changes at each age bracket

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Sim disappears from lot; gravestone object appears on property
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Other Sims express grief through sad animations and moodlets; household continues without deceased

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: House improvements visible; upgraded furniture; larger lot appearance; luxury objects present
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Sim career advancement; increased access to better quality items; expanded social mobility

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Deteriorating lot appearance; basic/minimal furnishings; poor condition visual state of objects
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Sim stress increases; relationship quality degrades; limited activity options

environmental_storytelling.example: A Sim's home tells their story through accumulated objects—career trophies, posters, furniture choices reveal personality and economic status
Career uniforms, formal wear, and custom clothing reflect Sim profession and social aspirations
Wedding rings, photos together, shared furniture arrangements communicate Sim relationship status and family bonds
Dirty/messy environments, unwashed appearance, low energy animations communicate neglect and poor living conditions
Suburban setting establishes American consumer culture context; lot sizes and locations imply socioeconomic stratification

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Leaves lot during work hours; returns with job-specific uniform or briefcase; displays career-related thought bubbles
Leaves for school during weekday mornings; returns in afternoon; can remain home on weekends/vacation
Autonomous positive actions; smiling animations; initiates social interactions; productive activity selection
Sad/angry animations; avoids social contact; selects comfort-seeking behaviors over productive tasks; reduced efficiency
Animated hug/kiss interactions; increased interaction frequency; collaborative activity participation
Avoidance animations; hostile interaction attempts; angry thought bubbles; reduced cooperation
Autonomous movement and interaction; responds to Sim commands; affects household dynamics and Sim mood

notes: The Sims (2000) uses dimetric projection visual presentation with isometric-like viewing angle. The game communicates world-state primarily through UI systems (need bars, aspiration meters) overlaid on simulation visuals, combined with behavioral animations and environmental customization. Political/faction communication is implicit rather than explicit—expressed through economic stratification (lot quality, furnishings) and career systems rather than overt faction visual markers. The game's satire of consumer culture is embedded in object desirability hierarchies and status signaling through possessions rather than explicit visual propaganda. Danger signals are system-mediated (need depletion) or environmental (fire); safety is conveyed through positive need indicators and relationship status.
